text: Randomized placebo-controlled clinical trial of a mindfulness app after minor surgery. n=42 adults. Participants used the app or a placebo app for 10 days. Primary outcomes were self-reported pain and analgesic use. Pain scores improved modestly in the intervention arm, while analgesic use did not clearly differ. Effect size was not reported.

draft.bottomLine: This trial suggests the mindfulness app may modestly improve reported pain after minor surgery, but the evidence is still limited by the small sample and the lack of a reported effect size.

whatPaperActuallyShows.interventionOrExposure: Mindfulness app versus placebo app for 10 days
whatPaperActuallyShows.observedResult: Pain scores improved modestly in the intervention arm, but analgesic use did not clearly differ

keyFindings.finding: Self-reported pain improved modestly with the mindfulness app.
keyFindings.effectDirection: Lower reported pain in the intervention arm
keyFindings.plainEnglishMeaning: The app may help some people feel better, but the study is too small to know how reliable or clinically important the effect is.

evidenceQuality.statisticalDetail: Primary outcomes were compared between groups

practicalUse.reasoning: This may be worth seeing as an adjunctive, low-risk option, but it is not strong enough to drive treatment decisions on its own.
practicalUse.caution: Do not change pain treatment plans based on this paper alone.
